name: Pleroma
description: The Pleroma, primarily originating in Gnostic traditions, refers to the totality of divine powers and attributes. It's envisioned as a spiritual universe, a realm of perfect being emanating from the ultimate, unknowable God (often referred to as the Monad). Within the Pleroma reside various aeons, divine beings representing different aspects of the Godhead. Gnostic systems often describe a cosmic drama where a disruption within the Pleroma, caused by Sophia (Wisdom), leads to the creation of the material world and the imprisonment of spiritual sparks within human beings. Proponents believe that through gnosis (spiritual knowledge), individuals can reconnect with their divine origins and return to the Pleroma. Some interpretations extend the concept beyond Gnosticism, applying it to similar ideas of a divine source or ultimate reality in other spiritual traditions. Mainstream scholarship views the Pleroma as a key concept within Gnostic cosmology, reflecting a complex theological system that attempts to explain the origin of evil and the path to salvation. Critics within mainstream religious studies analyze the Pleroma as a product of syncretism, blending elements of Greek philosophy, Judaism, and early Christianity, and often highlight its dualistic worldview, which posits a fundamental division between the spiritual and material realms. The emphasis on secret knowledge and individual salvation has also been a point of contention for some scholars.
subtitle: The realm of divine fullness.

claims.claim: The Pleroma is the source of all existence, a realm of perfect divine beings.
claims.evidence: Gnostic texts describe the Pleroma as emanating from the Monad and containing all divine attributes.
claims.criticism: Mainstream scholarship views this as a mythological construct, a symbolic representation of theological concepts rather than a literal description of reality.

claims.claim: Human beings contain a spark of the divine Pleroma within them.
claims.evidence: Gnostic texts suggest that souls originate from the Pleroma and are trapped in the material world.
claims.criticism: Mainstream science and theology reject the idea of a divine spark within humans, attributing consciousness and self-awareness to biological and psychological processes.

claims.claim: Gnosis (spiritual knowledge) is the key to returning to the Pleroma.
claims.evidence: Gnostic texts emphasize the importance of inner knowledge and self-discovery as the path to salvation.
claims.criticism: Mainstream religious institutions often criticize the Gnostic emphasis on individual knowledge over established religious authority and dogma.

keyFigures.name: Valentinus
keyFigures.role: Prominent Gnostic teacher who developed a complex Pleroma cosmology

keyFigures.name: Irenaeus of Lyons
keyFigures.role: Early Christian theologian who criticized Gnostic teachings on the Pleroma

timeline.event: Development of Gnostic systems with detailed descriptions of the Pleroma
Criticism of Gnostic Pleroma concepts by early Church Fathers

sources.author: Kurt Rudolph
sources.work: Gnosis: The Nature and History of Gnosticism
sources.title: Kurt Rudolph
